• Show All Code
  • Hide All Code

Parameterising the distribution of factor loadings for simulation

Ian Hussey

20 July 2026

Goal

Simulation studies of psychometric methods need a data-generating process (DGP) for item factor loadings. The companion analysis (analysis_item_correlations_factor_loadings.Rmd) builds a hierarchical mixture DGP that captures between-scale clustering and a discrete “stray” (cross-facet) item component. That model is faithful but has many moving parts (a scale level, a within-scale spread, a stray mixing weight, and a resampling step for scale composition), which makes it awkward to state in a methods section or to reason about analytically.

This file asks a narrower question: what single, off-the-shelf parametric distribution best describes the pooled distribution of standardized factor loadings, so a simulation can draw loadings with one line and two or three numbers? A single distribution deliberately ignores the scale-level structure (that is the mixture model’s job) and targets the marginal distribution of loadings — the quantity a simulation needs when items are drawn i.i.d.

Standardized loadings are bounded on the logical range [-1, 1], are typically concentrated around 0.4-0.7, and have a short left tail crossing into negative values. A distribution for them should respect that bounded, skewed shape. We therefore compare a ladder of candidates, including a scaled Beta, obtained by rescaling loadings from their [-1, 1] logical range onto the Beta’s [0, 1] support, and select the best by information criteria and distributional distance.

Dependencies

library(tidyverse)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
library(arrow)
library(lavaan)
library(sn)        # skew-normal / skew-t
library(moments)   # skewness / kurtosis

set.seed(42)

Get data

Trait scales only (Bainbridge, AIID scales, Brysbaert), matching the companion analysis. Attitudes are single-item and have no factor loadings.

dat_bainbridge <- read_csv("../../data/bainbridge et al. 2022/processed/data_processed_bainbridge_itemlevel_long.csv")

dat_brysbaert_long <- read_parquet("../../data/brysbaert et al. 2024/processed/data_processed_brysbaert_itemlevel_long.parquet")

dat_aiid_scales_long <- read_parquet("../../data/AIID/processed/data_processed_aiid_scales_itemlevel_long.parquet")

dat <- dat_bainbridge |>
  bind_rows(dat_aiid_scales_long) |>
  bind_rows(dat_brysbaert_long)

Completeness filter

Drop each (participant, scale) record that answered fewer than 80% of the scale’s items, applied uniformly across all datasets (see the companion analysis for the rationale).

min_completeness <- 0.80

scale_n_items <- dat |>
  count(id, scale, source, name = "n_answered") |>
  group_by(scale, source) |>
  summarize(n_items = median(n_answered), .groups = "drop")

complete_records <- dat |>
  count(id, scale, source, name = "n_answered") |>
  left_join(scale_n_items, by = c("scale", "source")) |>
  filter(n_answered >= min_completeness * n_items) |>
  select(id, scale, source)

dat <- dat |>
  semi_join(complete_records, by = c("id", "scale", "source"))

Single-factor CFA loadings

For each (scale, source) we fit a single-factor CFA (lavaan, ML, std.lv = TRUE) and extract the standardized loadings, exactly as in the companion analysis, with one deliberate difference: residual item-level missingness (left by the 80% gate) is handled by listwise deletion rather than FIML. FIML (missing = "ml") triggers a hard segfault inside lavaan’s EM covariance-inverse step on a couple of the larger, near-collinear datasets; listwise uses the same estimator and, after the 80% completeness gate, discards little data. A single-factor model needs >= 3 items, so 1-2 item scales return no loadings; the global factor sign is reflected so that “negative loading” always means “item opposes the scale’s majority direction”. Alongside the loadings, each scale’s item count (n_items) and raw Cronbach’s alpha (alpha, computed on the same item matrix) are recorded and summarised below.

# Cronbach's alpha on the item matrix. Items are already correctly keyed
# upstream, so check.keys = FALSE gives plain (raw) alpha for the scale as
# scored. NA for < 2 items or on estimation failure.
calc_alpha <- function(w) {
  if (ncol(w) < 2) return(NA_real_)
  fit <- tryCatch(
    suppressWarnings(psych::alpha(as.data.frame(w), check.keys = FALSE,
                                  warnings = FALSE)),
    error = function(e) NULL)
  if (is.null(fit)) NA_real_ else fit$total$raw_alpha
}

fit_cfa <- function(df) {
  w <- df |>
    dplyr::select(id, item, response) |>
    tidyr::pivot_wider(names_from = item, values_from = response,
                       values_fn = function(x) x[1]) |>
    dplyr::select(-id)

  item_names <- colnames(w)
  alpha_val  <- calc_alpha(w)
  empty <- tibble(n_obs = NA_integer_, n_items = ncol(w), alpha = alpha_val,
                  loadings = list(tibble(item = character(), loading = numeric())))

  if (ncol(w) < 3) return(empty)

  colnames(w) <- paste0("i", seq_along(item_names))
  model <- paste("f =~", paste(colnames(w), collapse = " + "))

  fit <- tryCatch(
    suppressWarnings(lavaan::cfa(model, data = as.data.frame(w),
                                 std.lv = TRUE, estimator = "ML",
                                 missing = "listwise")),
    error = function(e) NULL)
  if (is.null(fit) || !lavaan::lavInspect(fit, "converged")) return(empty)

  std <- tryCatch(
    lavaan::standardizedSolution(fit) |>
      dplyr::filter(op == "=~") |>
      dplyr::pull(est.std),
    error = function(e) NULL)
  if (is.null(std) || !all(is.finite(std))) return(empty)

  if (sum(std < 0) > sum(std > 0)) std <- -std

  tibble(n_obs = lavaan::lavInspect(fit, "nobs"),
         n_items = length(item_names),
         alpha = alpha_val,
         loadings = list(tibble(item = item_names, loading = std)))
}

cfa_loadings <- dat |>
  group_by(scale, source) |>
  group_modify(~ fit_cfa(.x)) |>
  ungroup()

cfa_loadings_long <- cfa_loadings |>
  dplyr::select(scale, source, loadings) |>
  tidyr::unnest(loadings) |>
  filter(is.finite(loading))

Per-scale item counts and reliability

Distribution of the number of items and Cronbach’s alpha across the 206 scales that contribute loadings (min, percentiles, mean, SD, max).

# min / percentiles / mean / sd / max summary of a numeric vector
summarise_dist <- function(x) {
  x <- x[is.finite(x)]
  tibble(n      = length(x),
         min    = min(x),
         p10    = unname(quantile(x, 0.10)),
         p25    = unname(quantile(x, 0.25)),
         median = median(x),
         mean   = mean(x),
         sd     = sd(x),
         p75    = unname(quantile(x, 0.75)),
         p90    = unname(quantile(x, 0.90)),
         max    = max(x))
}

scale_metrics <- cfa_loadings |> filter(!is.na(n_obs))

bind_rows(
  summarise_dist(scale_metrics$n_items) |>
    mutate(metric = "n_items per scale", .before = 1),
  summarise_dist(scale_metrics$alpha) |>
    mutate(metric = "Cronbach's alpha per scale", .before = 1)
) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 3))) |>
  kable(caption = paste("Per-scale item counts and Cronbach's alpha across the",
                        "scales contributing loadings.")) |>
  kable_classic(full_width = FALSE)
Per-scale item counts and Cronbach’s alpha across the scales contributing loadings.
metric n min p10 p25 median mean sd p75 p90 max
n_items per scale 206 3.000 5.000 9.000 13.000 18.364 14.977 21.000 39.000 90.000
Cronbach’s alpha per scale 206 0.556 0.748 0.803 0.853 0.846 0.076 0.895 0.938 0.994 
# n items per scale (right-skewed 3-90, so log10 x-axis)
ggplot(scale_metrics, aes(n_items)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "grey80", colour = "white") +
  scale_x_log10(breaks = c(3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 50, 90)) +
  labs(x = "Items per scale (log scale)", y = "N scales") +
  theme_linedraw()

# Cronbach's alpha per scale
ggplot(scale_metrics, aes(alpha)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.02, boundary = 0, fill = "grey80", colour = "white") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.70, linetype = "dashed", colour = "red") +
  labs(x = "Cronbach's alpha per scale", y = "N scales",
       caption = "Dashed line = conventional 0.70 threshold.") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0.5, 1)) +
  theme_linedraw()

Estimation base: scales and participants

The loadings summarised below are not drawn from one big pooled model; each is a standardized loading from a separate single-factor CFA fitted to one (scale, source) dataset. This section describes how many scales and participants sit behind them. n_obs is the number of participants each CFA actually used — the listwise complete-case count after the 80% completeness gate (lavaan’s nobs); it varies widely because the contributing samples do.

# converged datasets carry a non-missing n_obs; label by originating dataset
scale_base <- cfa_loadings |>
  filter(!is.na(n_obs)) |>
  mutate(dataset = dplyr::case_when(source == "Bainbridge" ~ "Bainbridge",
                                    source == "AIID"       ~ "AIID",
                                    TRUE                    ~ "Brysbaert"))

# participants contributing to at least one converged scale, by dataset
participants_by_dataset <- dat |>
  semi_join(distinct(scale_base, scale, source), by = c("scale", "source")) |>
  mutate(dataset = dplyr::case_when(source == "Bainbridge" ~ "Bainbridge",
                                    source == "AIID"       ~ "AIID",
                                    TRUE                    ~ "Brysbaert")) |>
  group_by(dataset) |>
  summarize(participants = n_distinct(id), .groups = "drop")

n_participants_total <- dat |>
  semi_join(distinct(scale_base, scale, source), by = c("scale", "source")) |>
  distinct(id) |>
  nrow()

Overall:

tibble(
  n_scales               = nrow(scale_base),
  n_loadings             = nrow(cfa_loadings_long),
  n_participants_distinct = n_participants_total,
  items_min              = min(scale_base$n_items),
  items_median           = median(scale_base$n_items),
  items_max              = max(scale_base$n_items),
  N_per_scale_min        = min(scale_base$n_obs),
  N_per_scale_median     = median(scale_base$n_obs),
  N_per_scale_max        = max(scale_base$n_obs)
) |>
  kable(caption = "Scales, loadings, and participants behind the loading distribution.") |>
  kable_classic(full_width = FALSE)
Scales, loadings, and participants behind the loading distribution.
n_scales n_loadings n_participants_distinct items_min items_median items_max N_per_scale_min N_per_scale_median N_per_scale_max
206 3783 196769 3 13 90 99 468 19330

By originating dataset:

scale_base |>
  group_by(dataset) |>
  summarize(n_scales    = n(),
            n_loadings  = sum(n_items),
            N_min       = min(n_obs),
            N_median    = median(n_obs),
            N_max       = max(n_obs),
            .groups = "drop") |>
  left_join(participants_by_dataset, by = "dataset") |>
  dplyr::select(dataset, n_scales, n_loadings, participants,
                N_min, N_median, N_max) |>
  kable(caption = paste("Per-CFA participant N (n_obs) is the listwise",
                        "complete-case count; 'participants' is the distinct",
                        "ids contributing to that dataset's scales.")) |>
  kable_classic(full_width = FALSE)
Per-CFA participant N (n_obs) is the listwise complete-case count; ‘participants’ is the distinct ids contributing to that dataset’s scales.
dataset n_scales n_loadings participants N_min N_median N_max
AIID 10 145 46209 4448 4565.5 4734
Bainbridge 54 384 1027 246 781.0 1027
Brysbaert 142 3254 149533 99 416.0 19330

Per-scale sample sizes span 99 to 19,330 participants (median 468), across 206 scales from 196769 distinct participants. Item counts per scale range from 3 to 90 (median 13). Because loadings come from many independent scales of unequal size, the pooled distribution below is an unweighted pool of item loadings — each item counts once regardless of its scale’s N — which is the right target for a DGP that draws items, not scales.

ggplot(scale_base, aes(n_obs)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, fill = "grey80", colour = "white") +
  scale_x_log10(labels = scales::comma) +
  labs(x = "Participants per scale (n_obs, log scale)", y = "N scales") +
  theme_linedraw()

Empirical distribution of loadings

lambda_raw <- cfa_loadings_long$loading

# Standardized loadings are logically in [-1, 1]; clamp any Heywood cases just
# inside the open interval so atanh() and the Beta rescale are defined.
eps <- 1e-3
n_clamped <- sum(abs(lambda_raw) >= 1 - eps)
lambda <- pmin(pmax(lambda_raw, -(1 - eps)), 1 - eps)
n_load <- length(lambda)

tibble(
  n_loadings   = n_load,
  n_scales     = nrow(distinct(cfa_loadings_long, scale, source)),
  n_clamped    = n_clamped,
  mean         = mean(lambda),
  sd           = sd(lambda),
  skew         = moments::skewness(lambda),
  ex_kurtosis  = moments::kurtosis(lambda) - 3,
  min          = min(lambda),
  max          = max(lambda),
  prop_negative = mean(lambda < 0)
) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 3))) |>
  kable() |>
  kable_classic(full_width = FALSE)
n_loadings n_scales n_clamped mean sd skew ex_kurtosis min max prop_negative
3783 206 0 0.509 0.213 -0.576 -0.054 -0.305 0.986 0.017 
ggplot(tibble(lambda), aes(lambda)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), binwidth = 0.05,
                 boundary = 0, fill = "grey80", colour = "white") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "red") +
  labs(x = "Standardized single-factor loading", y = "Density") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-1, 1)) +
  theme_linedraw()

The pooled loadings are bounded, unimodal, concentrated around ~0.5, and left-skewed with a short tail into negative values. A good single distribution must reproduce the location, the spread, that left skew, and the small negative-tail mass.

Candidate single-distribution models

We fit six single distributions by maximum likelihood. Three act directly on the loading scale (normal, skew-normal, skew-t); two act on the Fisher-z transform z = atanh(lambda) (unbounded, so a normal/skew-normal there maps back to a bounded, skewed shape on the loading scale); and the scaled Beta rescales loadings onto [0, 1] via p = (lambda + 1) / 2 and fits Beta(a, b).

To compare models fitted on different scales fairly, every log-likelihood is expressed on the loading scale, adding the Jacobian of each transform:

  • Fisher-z models: log f_lambda = log f_z(atanh(lambda)) - log(1 - lambda^2).
  • Scaled Beta: log f_lambda = log dbeta((lambda+1)/2, a, b) + log(1/2).

With all log-likelihoods on a common scale, AIC and BIC are comparable across the whole ladder. We also report the one-sample Kolmogorov-Smirnov distance between each fitted CDF and the empirical loadings, and the mean / SD / negative proportion implied by drawing from each fitted model.

models <- list()

## 1. Normal on raw loadings
m_ <- mean(lambda); s_ <- sd(lambda)
models$normal <- list(
  k = 2, ll = sum(dnorm(lambda, m_, s_, log = TRUE)),
  dens = function(x) dnorm(x, m_, s_),
  cdf  = function(x) pnorm(x, m_, s_),
  qf   = function(p) qnorm(p, m_, s_),
  rng  = function(nn) rnorm(nn, m_, s_),
  par  = sprintf("mean = %.3f, sd = %.3f", m_, s_))

## 2. Skew-normal on raw loadings
dp_sn <- coef(selm(lambda ~ 1), param.type = "DP")
models$`skew-normal` <- list(
  k = 3, ll = sum(dsn(lambda, dp = dp_sn, log = TRUE)),
  dens = function(x) dsn(x, dp = dp_sn),
  cdf  = function(x) psn(x, dp = dp_sn),
  qf   = function(p) qsn(p, dp = dp_sn),
  rng  = function(nn) as.numeric(rsn(nn, dp = dp_sn)),
  par  = sprintf("xi = %.3f, omega = %.3f, alpha = %.3f",
                 dp_sn[1], dp_sn[2], dp_sn[3]))

## 3. Skew-t on raw loadings
dp_st <- tryCatch(coef(selm(lambda ~ 1, family = "ST"), param.type = "DP"),
                  error = function(e) NULL)
if (!is.null(dp_st)) models$`skew-t` <- list(
  k = 4, ll = sum(dst(lambda, dp = dp_st, log = TRUE)),
  dens = function(x) dst(x, dp = dp_st),
  cdf  = function(x) pst(x, dp = dp_st),
  qf   = function(p) qst(p, dp = dp_st),
  rng  = function(nn) as.numeric(rst(nn, dp = dp_st)),
  par  = sprintf("xi = %.3f, omega = %.3f, alpha = %.3f, nu = %.0f",
                 dp_st[1], dp_st[2], dp_st[3], dp_st[4]))

## 4. Normal on Fisher-z  (Jacobian: -log(1 - lambda^2))
z <- atanh(lambda); mz <- mean(z); sz <- sd(z)
jac_z <- -log(1 - lambda^2)
z_clamp <- function(x) atanh(pmin(pmax(x, -(1 - eps)), 1 - eps))
models$`fisher-z normal` <- list(
  k = 2, ll = sum(dnorm(z, mz, sz, log = TRUE) + jac_z),
  dens = function(x) dnorm(atanh(x), mz, sz) / (1 - x^2),
  cdf  = function(x) pnorm(z_clamp(x), mz, sz),
  qf   = function(p) tanh(qnorm(p, mz, sz)),
  rng  = function(nn) tanh(rnorm(nn, mz, sz)),
  par  = sprintf("mean_z = %.3f, sd_z = %.3f", mz, sz))

## 5. Skew-normal on Fisher-z
dp_zsn <- coef(selm(z ~ 1), param.type = "DP")
models$`fisher-z skew-normal` <- list(
  k = 3, ll = sum(dsn(z, dp = dp_zsn, log = TRUE) + jac_z),
  dens = function(x) dsn(atanh(x), dp = dp_zsn) / (1 - x^2),
  cdf  = function(x) psn(z_clamp(x), dp = dp_zsn),
  qf   = function(p) tanh(qsn(p, dp = dp_zsn)),
  rng  = function(nn) tanh(as.numeric(rsn(nn, dp = dp_zsn))),
  par  = sprintf("xi = %.3f, omega = %.3f, alpha = %.3f",
                 dp_zsn[1], dp_zsn[2], dp_zsn[3]))

## 6. Scaled Beta: p = (lambda + 1)/2 ~ Beta(a, b); Jacobian log(1/2)
p_beta <- (lambda + 1) / 2
nll_beta <- function(par) -sum(dbeta(p_beta, exp(par[1]), exp(par[2]), log = TRUE))
mm <- mean(p_beta); vv <- var(p_beta); cc <- mm * (1 - mm) / vv - 1
opt <- optim(log(c(mm * cc, (1 - mm) * cc)), nll_beta, method = "BFGS")
a_beta <- exp(opt$par[1]); b_beta <- exp(opt$par[2])
models$`scaled beta` <- list(
  k = 2, ll = sum(dbeta(p_beta, a_beta, b_beta, log = TRUE) + log(0.5)),
  dens = function(x) dbeta((x + 1) / 2, a_beta, b_beta) * 0.5,
  cdf  = function(x) pbeta((x + 1) / 2, a_beta, b_beta),
  qf   = function(p) 2 * qbeta(p, a_beta, b_beta) - 1,
  rng  = function(nn) 2 * rbeta(nn, a_beta, b_beta) - 1,
  par  = sprintf("shape1 = %.3f, shape2 = %.3f", a_beta, b_beta))

Fit comparison

set.seed(1)
fit_tab <- purrr::imap_dfr(models, function(mo, nm) {
  ks  <- suppressWarnings(ks.test(lambda, mo$cdf)$statistic)
  sim <- mo$rng(200000)
  tibble(model = nm, n_params = mo$k, logLik = mo$ll,
         AIC = -2 * mo$ll + 2 * mo$k,
         BIC = -2 * mo$ll + mo$k * log(n_load),
         KS = unname(ks),
         implied_mean = mean(sim), implied_sd = sd(sim),
         implied_prop_neg = mean(sim < 0),
         params = mo$par)
}) |>
  arrange(AIC) |>
  mutate(dAIC = AIC - min(AIC), dBIC = BIC - min(BIC))

fit_tab |>
  dplyr::select(model, n_params, logLik, AIC, dAIC, BIC, dBIC, KS,
                implied_mean, implied_sd, implied_prop_neg) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 3))) |>
  kable(caption = paste0("Single-distribution fits to ", n_load,
    " loadings, all on the loading scale. Empirical: mean = ",
    round(mean(lambda), 3), ", sd = ", round(sd(lambda), 3),
    ", prop negative = ", round(mean(lambda < 0), 3), ".")) |>
  kable_classic(full_width = FALSE)
Single-distribution fits to 3783 loadings, all on the loading scale. Empirical: mean = 0.509, sd = 0.213, prop negative = 0.017.
model n_params logLik AIC dAIC BIC dBIC KS implied_mean implied_sd implied_prop_neg
scaled beta 2 651.219 -1298.438 0.000 -1285.961 0.000 0.018 0.508 0.211 0.017
fisher-z skew-normal 3 649.617 -1293.233 5.205 -1274.518 11.443 0.017 0.509 0.213 0.018
skew-normal 3 640.880 -1275.760 22.678 -1257.045 28.916 0.015 0.509 0.214 0.021
skew-t 4 640.880 -1273.760 24.678 -1248.807 37.155 0.015 0.510 0.214 0.021
fisher-z normal 2 628.364 -1252.728 45.710 -1240.252 45.710 0.021 0.508 0.220 0.025
normal 2 488.800 -973.600 324.838 -961.124 324.838 0.054 0.508 0.213 0.008 
fit_tab |>
  dplyr::select(model, params) |>
  kable(caption = "Fitted parameters.") |>
  kable_classic(full_width = FALSE)
Fitted parameters.
model params
scaled beta shape1 = 11.775, shape2 = 3.837
fisher-z skew-normal xi = 0.351, omega = 0.402, alpha = 1.330
skew-normal xi = 0.764, omega = 0.332, alpha = -3.335
skew-t xi = 0.764, omega = 0.332, alpha = -3.335, nu = 8305306
fisher-z normal mean_z = 0.607, sd_z = 0.310
normal mean = 0.509, sd = 0.213

Fitted densities overlaid

grid <- seq(-0.999, 0.999, length.out = 400)
dens_df <- purrr::imap_dfr(models, function(mo, nm)
  tibble(model = nm, x = grid, density = mo$dens(grid)))

ggplot() +
  geom_histogram(data = tibble(lambda), aes(lambda, y = after_stat(density)),
                 binwidth = 0.05, boundary = 0, fill = "grey85", colour = "white") +
  geom_line(data = dens_df, aes(x, density, colour = model), linewidth = 0.7) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-1, 1)) +
  labs(x = "Standardized loading", y = "Density", colour = NULL) +
  theme_linedraw() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

# facetted, ordered best-AIC first, for a cleaner per-model read
dens_df |>
  mutate(model = factor(model, levels = fit_tab$model)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x, density)) +
  geom_histogram(data = tidyr::crossing(model = factor(fit_tab$model,
                                                       levels = fit_tab$model),
                                        tibble(lambda)),
                 aes(lambda, y = after_stat(density)),
                 binwidth = 0.05, boundary = 0, fill = "grey85",
                 colour = "white", inherit.aes = FALSE) +
  geom_line(colour = "firebrick", linewidth = 0.7) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40") +
  facet_wrap(~ model) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-1, 1)) +
  labs(x = "Standardized loading", y = "Density") +
  theme_linedraw()

Quantile fidelity

qs <- c(.01, .05, .10, .25, .50, .75, .90, .95, .99)
q_tab <- tibble(quantile = qs, empirical = quantile(lambda, qs))
for (nm in fit_tab$model) q_tab[[nm]] <- models[[nm]]$qf(qs)

q_tab |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 2))) |>
  kable(caption = "Model quantiles vs empirical quantiles (loading scale).") |>
  kable_classic(full_width = FALSE)
Model quantiles vs empirical quantiles (loading scale).
quantile empirical scaled beta fisher-z skew-normal skew-normal skew-t fisher-z normal normal
0.01 -0.03 -0.05 -0.06 -0.09 -0.09 -0.11 0.01
0.05 0.10 0.13 0.12 0.11 0.11 0.10 0.16
0.10 0.20 0.22 0.22 0.22 0.22 0.21 0.24
0.25 0.38 0.37 0.37 0.38 0.38 0.38 0.37
0.50 0.53 0.53 0.53 0.54 0.54 0.54 0.51
0.75 0.66 0.67 0.67 0.67 0.67 0.67 0.65
0.90 0.77 0.77 0.77 0.76 0.76 0.76 0.78
0.95 0.81 0.82 0.81 0.81 0.81 0.81 0.86
0.99 0.87 0.89 0.88 0.89 0.89 0.87 1.00

QQ plot for the best model

best_model <- fit_tab$model[1]
pp <- ppoints(n_load)

tibble(theoretical = models[[best_model]]$qf(pp),
       empirical   = quantile(lambda, pp)) |>
  ggplot(aes(theoretical, empirical)) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "red") +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4, size = 0.7) +
  coord_equal() +
  labs(x = paste0("Theoretical quantile (", best_model, ")"),
       y = "Empirical loading quantile",
       title = paste0("QQ: ", best_model)) +
  theme_linedraw()

Chosen model and simulation recipe

best <- fit_tab |> slice(1)

The best single distribution by AIC/BIC is the scaled beta (shape1 = 11.775, shape2 = 3.837), a two-parameter model. It is not rejected by the Kolmogorov-Smirnov distance (KS = 0.018, versus an approximate 0.05 critical value of 0.022 at this sample size) and it reproduces the empirical mean (0.51 vs 0.51), SD (0.21 vs 0.21), left skew, and negative-tail mass (0.017 vs 0.017).

Because its support is exactly the [-1, 1] logical range of a standardized loading, every draw is a valid loading — no truncation or rejection needed. The 3-4 parameter skew-normal / skew-t fit marginally closer in raw KS but are not preferred once their extra parameters are penalised (higher AIC and BIC), and the skew-t’s degrees of freedom diverge, i.e. no heavy tails are needed. The plain normal is decisively rejected: it cannot represent the left skew.

The reusable data-generating function

Following the tidy DGP style of simulation_template_estimation.qmd’s generate_data() — named arguments in, a tibble built and return()ed — the generator below draws one scale’s worth of item loadings. Call it with n_items to get a tibble of item and loading.

The model has just two parameters: the Beta shape parameters of the rescaled loading p = (loading + 1) / 2 ~ Beta(shape1, shape2), with the loading recovered as loading = 2 * p - 1. Fitted to the Granary trait corpus these are shape1 (α) = 11.77 and shape2 (β) = 3.84, and the function’s defaults are set to these values so it is self-contained and copy-pasteable. What the two numbers encode:

  • Location. The Beta mean is α / (α + β) = 0.754 on [0, 1], i.e. an expected loading of 2·mean − 1 = 0.508 on [-1, 1].
  • Shape and skew. Both shapes exceed 1, so the density is unimodal with an interior mode (no spike at a boundary). Because β < α the mode sits in the upper half — loading mode 2·(α−1)/(α+β−2) − 1 = 0.583 — and the longer tail runs toward the lower end, reproducing the left skew and the small negative tail (P(loading < 0) = P(Beta < 0.5) = 0.017).
  • Spread and concentration. The concentration α + β = 15.6 governs the spread; larger values pack loadings more tightly around the mean, smaller values spread them out.
  • Support. Beta lives on [0, 1], so 2·Beta − 1 lives on [-1, 1], the logical range of a standardized loading — every draw is admissible, with no truncation or rejection step.
# Generate one scale's worth of standardized factor loadings from the fitted
# scaled Beta model: loading = 2 * Beta(shape1, shape2) - 1.
#   n_items  number of item loadings to draw
#   shape1   Beta alpha on the rescaled loading p = (loading + 1)/2
#            (default 11.78, fitted to the Granary trait corpus)
#   shape2   Beta beta on p (default 3.84, fitted to the Granary trait corpus)
generate_loadings <- function(n_items,
                              shape1 = 11.78,
                              shape2 = 3.84) {

  loadings <-
    tibble(item    = seq_len(n_items),
           loading = 2 * rbeta(n = n_items, shape1 = shape1, shape2 = shape2) - 1)

  return(loadings)
}

Sanity check: simulate a large number of loadings from the defaults and compare their summary to the empirical loadings.

set.seed(2026)
sim <- generate_loadings(n_items = 200000)

tibble(
  quantity  = c("mean", "sd", "skew", "prop_negative",
                "q05", "q50", "q95"),
  simulated = c(mean(sim$loading), sd(sim$loading),
                moments::skewness(sim$loading), mean(sim$loading < 0),
                quantile(sim$loading, .05), quantile(sim$loading, .50),
                quantile(sim$loading, .95)),
  empirical = c(mean(lambda), sd(lambda), moments::skewness(lambda),
                mean(lambda < 0),
                quantile(lambda, .05), quantile(lambda, .50),
                quantile(lambda, .95))
) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 3))) |>
  kable() |>
  kable_classic(full_width = FALSE)
quantity simulated empirical
mean 0.508 0.509
sd 0.212 0.213
skew -0.547 -0.576
prop_negative 0.017 0.017
q05 0.124 0.104
q50 0.530 0.534
q95 0.815 0.814

Predicted quantities

pred <- tibble(
  quantity = c("expected loading",
               "5th percentile loading",
               "50th percentile (median) loading",
               "95th percentile loading",
               "central 90% interval",
               "P(loading < 0)"),
  value = c(sprintf("%.3f", mean(sim$loading)),
            sprintf("%.3f", models[[best_model]]$qf(0.05)),
            sprintf("%.3f", models[[best_model]]$qf(0.50)),
            sprintf("%.3f", models[[best_model]]$qf(0.95)),
            sprintf("[%.3f, %.3f]", models[[best_model]]$qf(0.05),
                    models[[best_model]]$qf(0.95)),
            sprintf("%.3f", models[[best_model]]$cdf(0)))
)

pred |>
  kable() |>
  kable_classic(full_width = FALSE)
quantity value
expected loading 0.508
5th percentile loading 0.126
50th percentile (median) loading 0.531
95th percentile loading 0.815
central 90% interval [0.126, 0.815]
P(loading < 0) 0.017

Implied scale reliability (Cronbach’s alpha)

Because the DGP specifies the distribution of standardized loadings, it also implies a distribution of Cronbach’s alpha for any given number of items — with no extra assumptions beyond the single-factor measurement model the loadings come from. This lets a simulation set a target reliability by choosing the number of items, or check that a chosen n_items yields realistic alphas.

Derivation

Under a standardized single-factor model, item \(i\) has unit variance and loads \(\lambda_i\) on the common factor, so \(\operatorname{Cov}(i,j) = \lambda_i\lambda_j\) for \(i \neq j\). For a \(k\)-item unit-weighted sum score,

\[\operatorname{Var}\!\left(\textstyle\sum_i Y_i\right) \;=\; k \;+\; S_1^2 - S_2, \qquad S_1 = \sum_i \lambda_i,\quad S_2 = \sum_i \lambda_i^2,\]

and coefficient alpha is

\[\alpha \;=\; \frac{k}{k-1}\left(1 - \frac{k}{k + S_1^2 - S_2}\right) \;=\; \frac{k}{k-1}\cdot\frac{S_1^2 - S_2}{\,k + S_1^2 - S_2\,}.\]

Drawing the \(k\) loadings i.i.d. from the fitted scaled Beta with mean \(\mu = \mathbb{E}[\lambda]\), the expected off-diagonal covariance sum is \(\mathbb{E}[S_1^2 - S_2] = k(k-1)\mu^2\) — the loading variance cancels — which gives the closed-form population alpha

\[\alpha(k) \;=\; \frac{k\,\mu^2}{1 + (k-1)\,\mu^2},\]

the classic Spearman-Brown form with mean inter-item correlation \(\bar r = \mu^2\). Under the fitted scaled Beta the mean loading is \(\mu = 2\cdot\tfrac{\alpha_\beta}{\alpha_\beta + \beta_\beta} - 1\).

# mean loading and mean inter-item correlation implied by the fitted scaled Beta
mu  <- 2 * a_beta / (a_beta + b_beta) - 1
mu2 <- mu^2   # implied mean inter-item correlation r_bar

# closed-form population alpha for k items
alpha_closed_form <- function(k, r = mu2) k * r / (1 + (k - 1) * r)

# exact model-implied alpha for a specific drawn loading vector (keeps the
# loading heterogeneity that the closed form averages over)
alpha_from_loadings <- function(lambda) {
  k <- length(lambda); S1 <- sum(lambda); S2 <- sum(lambda^2)
  (k / (k - 1)) * ((S1^2 - S2) / (k + S1^2 - S2))
}

The mean loading is 0.508, so the implied mean inter-item correlation is \(\bar r = \mu^2 =\) 0.258.

Implied alpha at the observed item-count percentiles

For each percentile of the observed per-scale item count, the table gives the closed-form alpha and a Monte-Carlo alpha (mean and central 90% interval over 20,000 simulated scales drawn from generate_loadings(), which — unlike the closed form — retains the item-to-item loading heterogeneity within a scale).

set.seed(2027)

pctls       <- c(0, 0.10, 0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 0.90, 1)
pctl_labels <- c("min", "p10", "p25", "median", "p75", "p90", "max")
k_grid      <- as.integer(round(quantile(scale_metrics$n_items, pctls)))

implied_alpha <- purrr::pmap_dfr(
  list(pctl_labels, k_grid),
  function(lab, k) {
    sims <- replicate(20000, alpha_from_loadings(generate_loadings(k)$loading))
    tibble(percentile        = lab,
           n_items           = k,
           alpha_closed_form = alpha_closed_form(k),
           alpha_mc_mean     = mean(sims),
           alpha_mc_q05      = unname(quantile(sims, 0.05)),
           alpha_mc_q95      = unname(quantile(sims, 0.95)))
  })

implied_alpha |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 3))) |>
  kable(caption = paste("Cronbach's alpha implied by the loading DGP at the",
                        "observed per-scale item-count percentiles.")) |>
  kable_classic(full_width = FALSE)
Cronbach’s alpha implied by the loading DGP at the observed per-scale item-count percentiles.
percentile n_items alpha_closed_form alpha_mc_mean alpha_mc_q05 alpha_mc_q95
min 3 0.511 0.484 0.171 0.736
p10 5 0.635 0.611 0.377 0.787
p25 9 0.758 0.745 0.604 0.848
median 13 0.819 0.811 0.717 0.880
p75 21 0.880 0.876 0.825 0.915
p90 39 0.931 0.930 0.908 0.948
max 90 0.969 0.969 0.963 0.974

Implied vs observed

The curve is the closed-form \(\alpha(k)\); the band is the DGP’s central 90% interval (Monte Carlo); the points are the observed per-scale alphas. The DGP tracks the observed alphas through the mid-range of item counts, but the marginal, i.i.d.-item model under-predicts reliability for the shortest scales and slightly over-predicts it for the longest ones. Both gaps reflect the scale-level structure this single-distribution model deliberately omits: short scales used in practice are curated so their few items cohere (higher loadings than a random marginal draw), whereas the longest instruments are the multidimensional ones that carry the low and stray loadings — the very structure captured by the hierarchical mixture DGP in analysis_item_correlations_factor_loadings.Rmd.

k_seq <- seq(3, max(scale_metrics$n_items), length.out = 40) |> round() |> unique()

alpha_band <- purrr::map_dfr(k_seq, function(k) {
  sims <- replicate(3000, alpha_from_loadings(generate_loadings(k)$loading))
  tibble(n_items = k,
         mid = mean(sims),
         lo  = unname(quantile(sims, 0.05)),
         hi  = unname(quantile(sims, 0.95)))
})

ggplot() +
  geom_ribbon(data = alpha_band, aes(n_items, ymin = lo, ymax = hi),
              fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.18) +
  geom_line(data = tibble(n_items = k_seq, alpha = alpha_closed_form(k_seq)),
            aes(n_items, alpha), colour = "steelblue", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(data = scale_metrics, aes(n_items, alpha),
             alpha = 0.4, size = 1) +
  scale_x_log10(breaks = c(3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 50, 90)) +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, 1)) +
  labs(x = "Items per scale (log scale)",
       y = "Cronbach's alpha",
       caption = paste("Line = closed-form alpha(k). Band = DGP central 90%",
                       "(Monte Carlo). Points = observed per-scale alpha.")) +
  theme_linedraw()

Summary

The paragraph below is written from extracted variables (inline R), so it stays correct if the data or fit change; it can be copied into a simulation study’s method section.

Across 3783 standardized single-factor loadings from 206 (scale, source) datasets, the loadings have mean 0.51 (SD 0.21), are left-skewed (skew -0.58), and are negative 1.7% of the time. Comparing six single distributions on a common (loading) scale, the best-fitting parameterisation is a scaled Beta: rescale a loading to p = (loading + 1) / 2 and model p ~ Beta(11.77, 3.84), i.e. loading = 2 × Beta(11.77, 3.84) − 1. This two-parameter model has the lowest AIC and BIC of all candidates (including 3-4 parameter skew-normal and skew-t alternatives), is not rejected by a Kolmogorov-Smirnov test (KS = 0.018), reproduces the empirical mean, SD, skew and negative-loading rate, and because its support is the [-1, 1] logical range of a standardized loading guarantees admissible draws. To simulate a scale of loadings, call generate_loadings(n_items) (defined above), which draws loading = 2 × rbeta(n_items, shape1 = 11.77, shape2 = 3.84) − 1 with those fitted shapes as defaults. This single distribution intentionally omits between-scale clustering; when a simulation must reproduce scale-level structure (e.g. that negative loadings concentrate within particular multidimensional scales, or a tunable multidimensional-contamination severity), use the hierarchical mixture DGP in analysis_item_correlations_factor_loadings.Rmd instead.
